assignment.taskDescription: Analyze the deviation from Raoult's Law in a mixture of Ethanol and Acetone. Write a brief report (150-200 words) explaining why this specific mixture exhibits positive deviation, referencing intermolecular forces before and after mixing. Include one real-life example where such deviations are utilized or observed.
rubric: Correctly identifies the type of deviation as 'Positive' based on vapor pressure behavior (1 mark)
Accurately explains the breaking of strong hydrogen bonds in pure ethanol/acetone and formation of weaker dipole-dipole interactions upon mixing (2 marks)
Provides a logical connection between bond strength changes, endothermic enthalpy change ($\Delta H_{mix} > 0$), and volume expansion ($V_{mix} > \sum V_i$) (1 mark)
Includes at least one relevant real-life application or observation related to non-ideal solutions (e.g., azeotropes in distillation, boiling point elevation anomalies) (1 mark)
assignment.answerKey: The mixture of Ethanol and Acetone exhibits positive deviation from Raoult's Law. In pure ethanol, molecules are held together by strong hydrogen bonds. When acetone is added, it disrupts these H-bonds because the interaction between ethanol and acetone (dipole-dipole) is weaker than the original ethanol-ethanol or acetone-acetone interactions. This leads to an increase in vapor pressure above the ideal value ($P_{total} > P_1^0 x_1 + P_2^0 x_2$), a positive enthalpy of mixing, and volume expansion. A real-life example is found in fractional distillation where such mixtures form minimum boiling point azeotropes (like ethanol-water or acetone-ethanol), making complete separation by simple distillation impossible.
